KHANH NGUYEN: All right, so
I have my app running here.

Let's go ahead and
submit some feedback.
This yellow-- not feeling it.

So let's say that I really
don't like this yellow.
It's really jarring.
It really is.

So let's say that I
prefer for it to be blue.
All right.
Yes, please.
Change the app color to blue.

And you'll see that the app
has now shifted its theme
color to blue.

Hi, I'm Khanh, a DevRel engineer
on the Flutter team at Google.

ANDREW PEYNADO: Hey, I'm
Andrew, a product manager
on the Firebase team at Google.

KHANH NGUYEN: Have you
heard about these AI agents?

Maybe your boss
said that you need
to integrate one into your app.

As an engineer, one
of the first things

that I looked up after
learning about AI agents was,
how do I build one?

Google Search seems to
indicate that many of you
are asking the same questions.

So in this video,
we're going to show you

how to build a multimodal
agentic app with Flutter
and Vertex AI in Firebase.

We want you to leave this video
feeling like building agentic

apps is just as straightforward
as building any other app that
you're used to .

And hopefully you'll think of
agentic features and experiences

as another tool in your
app development toolbox.
OK.
So what's on the agenda, Andrew?

ANDREW PEYNADO:
First, we'll chat

about generative
AI and agents, just

to make sure we're
all on the same page.

Then we'll do a run-through
of Flutter and Firebase,
the tools we're using today.

And finally, we're going to
jump into the code for the app

that you just saw at
the start of the video.
We'll get to agents soon.

But first, let's
take a step back

and look at one of the
foundational building
blocks for agents--
generative AI.

GenAI allows you to create
new and innovative content

and experiences by prompting
large language models.

With prompting, you
can turn a few words

into an explanation or code,
an image, or even a video.

Gemini is Google's
multimodal AI model.

Gemini 2.0 and 2.5 are
Google's latest LLMs.

It improves on Gemini
1.5's overall quality,

has function calling, and
supports multimodal live,

which lets users have
low-latency, bidirectional voice

and video interactions
with Gemini.

We've already seen a
lot of examples and code

on how to generate content
with language models,

so let's shift our
focus towards AI agents.
What is it?

An agent is an
application that attempts

to achieve a goal by observing
the world and acting upon it,

using the tools that
it has at its disposal.
Here's how it looks in practice.

An agent is made up of
three key components--

a language model for
reasoning, like Gemini,

tools that enable it to
interact with the outside world,

whether that's making an
API call to retrieve data
or to take some kind of action.

Keep in mind the phrase
"tool" and "function calling"

are often used interchangeably
and represent the same concept.

Finally, it also processes
information, makes decisions,

and continues to iterate
until its goal is reached

or a stopping point
criteria is met.

Now that we know
what agents are,

let's take a look at how to
build them into your apps
with Flutter and Firebase.

If you're a Flutter
developer, chances

are you've already
used a Firebase
service in one of your apps.

But if this is the first
time building with Firebase,

it's an all-in-one app
development platform

that spans the entire
development lifecycle.

You can use it to build and run
modern AI-powered experiences.

On the build side, you can
integrate AI capabilities
into your applications.

On the run side, you can
use AI assistive tools

to optimize your app's
quality and experience.

Vertex AI in Firebase, which
we'll call the Vertex SDK,

is a part of the broader
Firebase family of services.

It's an easy way to add
GenAI to mobile and web apps
in production.
Yep, it's in GA.

So you can add it
to your Flutter app

and ship it right
to the App Store.

The Vertex SDK allows
you to easily access

Gemini models using native
code and supports a variety

of features, such as multimodal
prompting, JSON output,

and schemas, function calling,
bidirectional streaming
and image generation.

It's natively integrated with
App Check, Remote Config,

and Cloud Storage, so you don't
have to wire them up yourself.

What makes it
exceptionally special

is its production-ready
architecture.

By providing a native
integration with App Check,

the Vertex SDK allows you
to protect your applications

from abuse by rejecting
illegitimate requests

and only allowing real users
to trigger Vertex API calls.

The Vertex SDK is available on
pub.dev as the Firebase Vertex
AI package.

So you can quickly add it
to your Flutter projects
by running a single command--

flutter pub add
firebase_vertexai.

If you can build a Flutter app,
you can use the Vertex SDK.

It's just another API call,
so why Flutter though?

KHANH NGUYEN:
Andrew, I mean, we're
on the Flutter YouTube channel.

I think everyone
already knows why.

But in case you're new to
the channel, first, welcome.
We're happy to have you here.

And second, Flutter is
the world's most popular

multiplatform app
development SDK.

It enables you to build
production-grade apps

and deploy to iOS, Android,
web, Windows, macOS, and Linux,
all from a single code base.

This means you can
minimize development time

and ship high-quality
apps faster.

Not to mention, using Flutter
and the Vertex SDK together

means that you don't have
to switch between languages
or frameworks.

You don't even have to switch
between projects or code bases.

All of your code and prompts
live within your Flutter
project, written in Dart.

Like Andrew said, if you
can build a Flutter app,

you can use the Vertex
SDK and vice versa.

They're better together, sort
of like macaroni and cheese.
ANDREW PEYNADO: OK.

Now that we have all
the context, let's jump
into building the app.

If you've used Flutter and
Firebase together before,

the setup process will
look very familiar.

You'll first want to make sure
that your dev environment is

set up with Flutter and
the Flutter fire CLI tool.

Then head on over
to the Firebase

Console at firebase.google.com
and create a project.

If you already have a project,
you're already one step ahead.

Then jump over to the
Vertex AI product page,

configure your billing account,
and enable the Vertex APIs.

That's as much as you'll have
to do in the Firebase Console.

Let's look at setting
up your Flutter project.

If you already have a Flutter
project you'd like to work with,
that's perfect.
You can use it.

If you're starting fresh,
you can create a new project

using the Flutter
create command.

Next, add the Firebase core
and Firebase Vertex AI packages
from pub.dev.

Finally, you'll want to
connect the Flutter app

to the Firebase project using
the flutterfire configure
command.

In your Flutter
code, you'll want

to import the Firebase packages
and initialize the Firebase App.

Now, if you're already
using Firebase in your app,

you probably already have
that initialize app code.

It's the same across all
of the Firebase packages.
That's it for the project setup.

Now you can make calls to
Gemini using the Vertex SDK
from within your Flutter app.

KHANH NGUYEN: Let
me tell you a story

about how I came up with the
concept for this demo app.

I previously worked
as a support engineer.

Whether you're the
user filing a complaint

or the engineer
supporting users,
you know how the process goes.

The user tries to
do something, but it

doesn't work the way
that they want it to,
so they open a ticket.

An engineer reviews it
and points to some docs,

or they ask for some
additional information.

The user responds with
the results or answers,

and then the back and forth
goes on for a few more cycles.

Finally, the ticket
is eventually closed.

This process takes a lot of time
from both the user and engineer.

What if the engineer misses
something and the user

configuration, or
perhaps a user overlooks
a step in the instructions?

What if we could insert an agent
in the middle of this process

to help both the user
and the engineer,

an agent that
processes user feedback

and takes action to make
changes in the app on behalf

of the user, collects all of
the necessary troubleshooting

information like device
Info, battery info, settings,

and change history, and helps
to prioritize and categorize

the feedback among all
of the other tickets?

Would an agent like
this help to minimize

the amount of back
and forth that

needs to happen between
the user and the engineer?

And that's how the agentic app
manager prototype was born.
Let's try this font here.
I don't like this Georgia font.
I want a more fun font.
So we'll wait for that.

And then you'll see, it prompts
you for a different font.

In this case, it's Caveat,
which is indeed a more fun
font, most certainly.
But now the font is too small.

So we'll say this font
size is way too small--
oops, not "but."
It needs to be about 30% bigger.
Yes, please.

And as you can see, it
makes the font much bigger,
and it's much more readable now.

Now finally, let's pretend this
page is scrolling really slowly.
And go ahead and submit.

And would you like to
file a feedback report?
Yes, please.
And so there you go.

And now you have the
full feedback report

with the user feedback as
well as the device and battery
information there.

And then there's the
screenshot as well.
So what did we just see?

The app lets you file
feedback by taking

a screenshot, letting
you annotate it,
and then write some text.

Then the agent does
some reasoning,

drawing conclusions as to
why you might be filing
that particular feedback.

Say you don't like the color
scheme and prefer blue.

The agent determines that
it has a tool to change that
and does it for you.
But of course, UX matters.

You wouldn't want the UI
to suddenly change out
from under you, right?

So the agent first uses another
tool to show an alert modal,

asking for confirmation before
actually changing the color.

And finally, if there are no
tools that the agent can use

or the setting change
request is denied,

the app automatically builds
a standardized feedback report

that helps the developer to
prioritize and troubleshoot

by collecting and summarizing
all the available device

and battery information,
providing an action history,

and generating tags along with
a suggested priority level.

You can see how the potential
use case for an agent like this
goes on and on.
Take a moment to think about it.

What are some of the other tools
that you can give to an agent
to make it even more useful?

Here's the architecture for
this agentic ad manager.

The Flutter app
provides user feedback

in the form of natural language
text and a marked-up screenshot.

The Vertex SDK sends
the input along

with the system instructions
that we've defined
to the Gemini 2.0 model.

The model then responds
with function calls

in order to fulfill its role of
being a friendly and helpful app
concierge.

You can see how this levels
up our use of generative AI.

Not only is Gemini
generating new content,

but it's actually taking
actions and making changes

in order to fulfill its goal
of being helpful to the user.

Looking at this
architecture diagram,

it looks like any other app
making API calls, right?

There are some data being
passed back and forth,
functions being called.

The real difference comes from,
one, Gemini's ability to reason,

understand, and process
unstructured, multimodal input,

and two, its ability
to determine an output
based on the prompting.

To build this type of
agentic functionality,

you first have to construct a
Firebase Vertex AI generative
model.

For this particular
prototype, we'll

configure the generative
model with Gemini 2.0 Flash.

Then we give Gemini a list
of tools or function calls
that it can use.

These tools can do things
like modify parts of the app,

retrieve device information,
and file a feedback report.

And finally, we define
a system instruction

to give the agent a persona
and goal, more specifically,

defining its goal of being
a friendly and helpful app

concierge, along with some
additional instructions
that we wanted to follow.

Once that's all configured,
we can start a chat session.

The nice thing about
these chat sessions

is that they store
the chat history
from our Gemini interactions.

Otherwise, we'd have to
implement our own chat history.

From there, whenever the
user hits submit feedback,

our callback sends
a message to Gemini

with a prompt along with the
feedback text and screenshot.

When response comes back,
we check whether Gemini
has made any function calls.

Let's say Gemini has issued
some tool calls, otherwise known
as function calls.

Keep in mind, Gemini
doesn't actually
execute the function itself.

It essentially
gives instructions
that say, do this thing.

But your app is ultimately
responsible for actually
doing the thing.

Gemini is sort of like
a backseat driver.

ANDREW PEYNADO: That's
actually helpful.

KHANH NGUYEN: Thanks for helping
me land that point, Andrew.

So with every response, we check
the function calls name and then

call the function that's
responsible for doing the thing.

Let's take a closer
look at two of the tools

that the agentic app
manager has at its disposal.

Each tool is defined as
a function declaration.

It's a declarative way of
describing what a tool does.

Each tool has a name
and a description,

along with a map of parameters
and their descriptions.

Remember that good
tool descriptions

are important because the
LLM uses them to determine
what the tool does.

Meanwhile, good
parameter descriptions

will help it to generate
more useful argument
values with each function call.

Here's the function declaration
for the app color tool.

This particular tool
requires three integers,

each representing the red,
green, and blue channels
in the RGB value.

And here's the function that
actually sets the app color.

We grab the RGB parameter
values that Gemini

has generated from the
function call object

and update the
app's color state.

And here's the
function declaration

for the tool, which asks
for user confirmation.

You can see the parameter
is a string that represents
the question to ask the user.

And for simplicity, the
answer to this question
needs to be either yes or no.

So when the Gemini response
invokes the Ask Confirmation

tool, we pull the question
that Gemini provided

as a parameter from the
function call object, drop it

into the function that
invokes the alert model,

take the response, which is true
or false, in place of yes or no,

and send it back to
Gemini as a chat message.

Assuming the user
responded yes, Gemini

should then respond with
another function call
to execute the change.

And that's how you
build an agentic app

with Vertex AI and
Firebase and Flutter,

implementing precisely
this architecture
diagram that we saw earlier.

The app sends text and a
screenshot to the Gemini model

that's been configured
with our system prompt.

Then Gemini responds by
calling various tools

to make changes and take action
in order to fulfill its goal.

A quick aside here before we

it's all built into the

Isn't that how mail

KHANH NGUYEN: Great

It's exciting because I

It's an image

but notice that it's an
Imagen model constructor.

Just like before, you specify

config.
Here, you can specify

the number of images that you
want back for each prompt.

At the time of this recording,
it'll default to one image,

but you can get as many as
four images if you wanted.

Then you can call the
generateImages method
with your prompt.

Once the response comes
back with the images,

you can grab the image
bytes and display them

in your app using a
memory image widget.

OK, but I do have one more
thing to show you today.

I was talking to one of our
awesome Firebase engineers,

and they mentioned a
really cool feature
that they were working on--

bidirectional audio streaming
using Gemini's live API.

In short, what if
I could literally

talk and have a conversation
with Gemini in real time?

I'd like to talk to
the manager, please.
Hey, Gemini.
GEMINI: Hello there.
How can I help you today?

KHANH NGUYEN: I don't like
the purple color of this app.

Can you change it
to a blue, please?

GEMINI: Sure, I can
help you with that.

I can change the
app color to blue.
Would you like me to do that?
KHANH NGUYEN: Yes, please.
Cool, thanks.
Can you also--
GEMINI: You're welcome.
Is there--

KHANH NGUYEN: --make the
font a serif font, please?

GEMINI: Sure, I can change
the font to a serif font.

Would you like me
to use Georgia,

which is a highly-readable
serif typeface designed
for screen use?
KHANH NGUYEN: Yes.
Never mind, I don't like that.

Can we go back to the
Inter font, please?
OK, thanks.
That's all I need.
GEMINI: You're welcome.
KHANH NGUYEN: Bye, Gemini.
GEMINI: Goodbye.
Have a great day.

ANDREW PEYNADO: Oh,
that's what you meant.
That's pretty cool.

This is a great time
for me to tell you

that real-time bidirectional
streaming is now
available in Preview.

So you can try it out

KHANH NGUYEN: And guess what?

The code follows the
same API structure

as the generative models
we've already used.

You swap out your

with a live generative
model constructor,

specify a model that has Gemini
Live API support, like Gemini

2.0 or Gemini 2.5, provide

which specifies the voice

And then you can reuse the

instructions.

From there, you can start

Of course, once you're done
building your agentic Flutter

app, you have quite a few
deployment options to pick from.

Believe it or not, we managed to
use three different generative
models from the Vertex SDK.

We first built our
agentic app manager

using a regular generative
model with Gemini 2.0.

Then we used the Imagen model to

And finally, we added

with the new Live generative

So, Andrew, what
are the high-level

takeaways that everyone should
take away from this video?

ANDREW PEYNADO: Well,

Number one, language
models like Gemini

enable us as developers to

Number two, Vertex
AI and Firebase

And number three,

KHANH NGUYEN: And
